name: Interphone Study
description: The Interphone Study was an international collaborative research project coordinated by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC), investigating the potential link between mobile phone use and certain types of head and neck cancers, specifically glioma and meningioma (brain tumors), acoustic neuroma (tumor of the auditory nerve), and parotid gland tumors. Conducted across 13 countries, it aimed to provide a comprehensive assessment of the association between mobile phone usage habits and the risk of these cancers. Proponents of a link between mobile phones and cancer often cite aspects of the Interphone Study, particularly subgroup analyses, as evidence supporting their claims. They might highlight specific findings regarding latency periods, heavy users, or tumors on the side of the head where phones were typically held. The mainstream scientific consensus, based on the overall findings of the Interphone Study and subsequent research, is that there is no established causal link between mobile phone use and cancer. While some studies, including aspects of Interphone, have shown statistically significant associations in certain subgroups, these findings are often attributed to methodological limitations such as recall bias (where participants inaccurately remember or report their mobile phone usage) or chance findings due to multiple comparisons. Critics of the study also point to the fact that the overall results did not demonstrate a consistent or dose-response relationship between mobile phone use and cancer risk. Furthermore, subsequent and larger studies, including cohort studies with prospective data collection, have largely failed to replicate the positive associations observed in some Interphone subgroups. The study's methodology, particularly the case-control design and reliance on self-reported data, has been subject to extensive scrutiny. Despite its limitations, the Interphone Study remains a significant piece of research in the ongoing investigation of potential health effects associated with mobile phone use.
subtitle: Mobile Phone Use and Cancer Risk

claims.claim: Mobile phone use significantly increases the risk of glioma, a type of brain tumor.
claims.evidence: Subgroup analyses within the Interphone Study showed elevated odds ratios for glioma in long-term, heavy mobile phone users, particularly those who held the phone on the same side of their head as the tumor.
claims.criticism: The overall Interphone Study results did not demonstrate a statistically significant association between mobile phone use and glioma. Subgroup analyses are prone to chance findings and may not reflect a true causal relationship. Recall bias and selection bias are also potential confounding factors.

claims.claim: Long latency periods (10+ years) of mobile phone use are required for cancer to develop.
claims.evidence: Some Interphone Study analyses suggested a stronger association between mobile phone use and cancer risk after a latency period of 10 years or more.
claims.criticism: Latency period analyses are complex and can be affected by changes in mobile phone technology and usage patterns over time. The observed association may be confounded by other factors that are correlated with both mobile phone use and cancer risk.

claims.claim: Acoustic neuroma risk is elevated with mobile phone use, especially on the side of the head where the phone is typically held.
claims.evidence: Some Interphone Study centers reported elevated odds ratios for acoustic neuroma associated with mobile phone use on the ipsilateral (same) side of the head.
claims.criticism: The association between mobile phone use and acoustic neuroma was not consistent across all Interphone Study centers, and the overall pooled analysis did not show a statistically significant association. Recall bias and small sample sizes may have influenced the findings in individual centers.
